explanation_md: x starts at 5 (> 0, prints 5), decrements to 3 (> 0, prints 3), decrements to 1 (> 0, prints 1), decrements to -1 (not > 0, loop exits). Three values are printed.

options.d: Infinite times — the condition is always true

explanation_md: n is already 10 when the loop is first evaluated. 10 < 5 is false, so the condition fails immediately and the body is never executed. A while loop can run zero times if the condition is false from the start.
i goes 1, 2, 3, 4 and sum accumulates 1+2+3+4 = 10. After i becomes 5, the condition 5 <= 4 is false and the loop exits, then 10 is printed.

options.b: A semicolon after the condition creates an empty infinite loop; the block below it is not the loop body
options.c: The loop variable must be declared inside the while condition
options.d: `count` must be initialized to 0 instead of 1

explanation_md: The semicolon immediately after `while (count < 3)` makes the while loop have an empty body. Since count never changes in that empty body, the loop spins forever on `count < 3` (which stays true). The block below is a regular compound statement that never executes. Removing the semicolon fixes it.

With `i < 5`, the loop stops after printing 4 (i becomes 5 and fails the check). Changing to `i <= 5` allows i=5 to pass the check, printing 5 before i becomes 6 and the loop exits. This is the classic off-by-one fix.
